speaker: interviewer
text: --with Lola Putt. Lola, when did you arrive at your first camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were talking about the roll calls, or what happened each morning, which camp are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about the Ravensbruck. I was there for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau. I'm sorry. I get confused. I'm very sorry. I'm a bit nervous today, so you have to forgive me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us what happened every morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning, they told us to get up and put us on attention. And it was rain, pouring. And the time they came to count, we were there for hours standing on attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did it start?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning. I don't know what time it was. I don't remember. Because they wanted to see if we are all there. They were afraid that maybe somebody escape or something. That was every morning they did this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't. There were Germans with dogs all around you everywhere, everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at these roll calls? What did you do? You just--

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when-- how long did you have to stand there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stood there for hours, 'til-- 'til-- because we were not the only barracks there. There were many. So the German officers, they used to come and count us. You know? There were too many of us. That was why you had to stand on attention so many hours. The rain and the cold was shocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the other women from in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all around the world where Jewish-- where people-- where the Germans went in the countries and from all around the world. Many from many other countries, not only from Greece. But there was many other-- from other countries as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you communicate with these other women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much, because I couldn't speak their language and they couldn't speak mine. But we-- one language we would understand. We suffered together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the only language we could understand, that we have to sleep five people in-- five women in-- in-- in one-- in one bed, like I said, what you call a bed, just a wooden-- wooden bed. There was one on top and one on the bottom. And there were five on the-- on the top ones and five on the bottom. Five women, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have extra blankets when it was winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No blankets at all. No blankets, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what food were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bit of soup and a little piece of bread every day. All day, that's all. Just a bit of soup and-- and a piece of bread. And you have to find your own dish to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to find a dish so you can get the soup. Some people used to do the things-- the-- you know, they didn't have any toilets. They used to do it in-- in the dishes. And one day I went and I found one dish for myself for taking soup. And it was so dirty, I vomit. I couldn't touch it, so I had to go without food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What toilet arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the blocks. They didn't have toilets. They had buckets. And when they were full, they used to tell us to take the buckets somewhere to dispose, and to bring them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have duties in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Duties?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work? Did you do any detailed work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First of all, I want to tell you what happened before I come to the work. I'll tell you that once a week the Germans-- the German officers-- they used to come in every barrack. They used to call it-- the day everybody has to be inside the blocks. Nobody was allowed to go out. They used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]. That is the name they gave, [NON-ENGLISH], that nobody's allowed to go out. And the Germans used to go block to block and tell the women to undressed-- I was, myself, in it-- to undress naked. And one by one, they used to pass through them naked, of course. And they were looking at you if you haven't got any disease or anything like that. The ones that didn't like it, they took the-- the number from the hand-- because we didn't have names, we only had numbers. Call us by numbers. And the ones that they didn't like it, that had something wrong with them, they took the number. And they put them in a room separate completely from other-- the others. And in the nighttime, they came with a truck that took them to the gas chamber. That was every week we have to do that. They came, and to be naked in front of them. Everybody had to go one by one. And they had to look at you up and down, if you are sick or not sick. And so they send you to the crematorium. And I tell you the truth, I was so terrified because I thought, I'll be next. That was-- they were doing this every-- every-- every-- every-- week, once a week. So I said, I don't know when-- when my time will come. I was terrified. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I didn't know when will be my-- my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to live with a fear, can you imagine? If some people doesn't know when they're dying, they are not scared. But if you know that you're going to die, your turn will come, it's a terrible thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, that you know the next morning you will be the one. They will take you to the crematorium if they don't like your body, if you are sick or not sick. That was every-- Pauline, that was every week the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they went to the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we knew it. Because they put it together to-- all those people they put it separate and we didn't see them anymore. We knew that they-- what they will do with those people. We knew it, that is why they're coming, to take the-- the-- the number, and to send them away to their death because they didn't like what-- maybe they saw a pimple, something on your body. Or they didn't like it the way you look. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine the-- the fear I had in my heart? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was terrible. How many times I said, God, let me go to sleep and never woke up. That was-- this-- it was in my mind that I'll going to be next. And to stay alive to die, I think somebody up there loves me. Did you work in Birkenau, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. In the morning, after the appell-- that's what they call it. If people don't know what it means, it was an attention. That was the attention. They took us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and march-- there were-- they were there, a big iron door with music-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] music. And people-- music is the same people like us, not German people. People that I knew it-- how-- about music.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to put on the door, on this big ironing door, and they used to play the music so to give us a little bit of oomph, you know?-- what do you call it?-- to go happily to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were standing there, soldiers with guns and-- and dogs. They march you, and they were next to us. From both sides, in the back, in the front, dogs and soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they took us to a field-- I don't know where it was, this place-- to a field. And to-- and they told us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to dig and make holes on the ground. What was the holes all about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime they need it for dead people to put in. Some-- sometimes they need it either when you are alive to shoot you and fall in. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was every day we used to do that. Every day. And one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was hungry. I was thirsty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw a bucket of water a little bit further up from us. And I said to myself, I wish I could have a bit of water. I'm so thirsty. I was working very hard. The-- the sun was on top of my head. I was hungry. I was tired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew I wasn't allowed to do that. I said to myself, ah, heck? They will-- you will kill me just the same, sooner or later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, and I drink-- I went for the bucket. And soon he saw me, he shot me. He shot me on the shoulder here that I've got a big scar there, a very big scar. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then when I was sitting there, I was doing nothing for the moment, because I couldn't move my hand. Blood was falling from my arm. And then we walked back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he see if you were alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to see, and he told me that you knew that you are not allowed to go to drink water. He told me. I was very angry. I was so-- I didn't care anymore what he does to me. I didn't care. I was so exhausted from all this-- this frustration, the hunger, the tired, and everything, I couldn't-- I couldn't take it anymore. So after that, when we finish work, the rest of the people, we went back, again, march to the camp. And when I went there, they have a little room there with medicine. I don't know what they had there. And I pour something on my shoulder. And it was boil like a kettle. Boil. I don't know what I put there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. It was painful, maybe because they pour something that it was so bad, the-- the-- the gunshot there, that I didn't want, maybe, I don't know, to have infections or something like that. That was-- it was something-- it was boiling.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't-- you can't imagine this-- the feeling, you know, the pain I had for-- for being shot. And that medicine they put in was a medicine-- look, they could take me-- they could put me in crematorium in the nighttime, you know? For them, it didn't matter. One less, you know? But they took me back to the camp, and they did this-- this put on me. And for a while I was not doing much, because I couldn't do it with-- with my arm. That was not straight away when the beginning was. That was already when I was there about a few months, you know, in-- in this Birkenau, you know? And then after that everybody-- I got a little bit better, you know? Then I start to work again the same thing, what-- what I was doing before. How did you survive the weekly inspections?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I suppose, a healthy person. Maybe I didn't have the sickness that they expect me to have. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was lucky. Like I said before to you, Pauline, somebody up there loves me to-- to be alive, because it was very hard to believe it that anybody could escape the Germans, anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to walk back by yourself that day or did others help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to work-- I have to walk. I have to walk back to the camp with a German next to me with a dog and a-- and-- and a rifle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the thoughts that you had that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, what can I say? I want to die. What can I say? I was-- I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't have food. I didn't have water. He shot me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What-- what else did I said? Well, if I have to die, I have to die. Who was with you from your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- my mother and my two little sisters went with my grandmothers. Straight away they went to crematorium. My brother and my father, they went to a different site, because the men are supposed to be separate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to work. And I didn't see my mother anymore and not my sisters anymore. Nobody. I didn't see anybody anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last time you were together with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in the train when we were going to Birkenau. But one day, girls took these buckets to throw somewhere away. And it-- it was a few men there working. They were doing something.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of them was my father. And he ask, do you know Lola? Tell her her father here. Please, I want to see her, not to talk to me, because if he sees me talk to him, [INAUDIBLE], he could be shot. I could be shot, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just tell her to come, to go pass through. I only want to see her. And girls came back, and they told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the buckets too, with another girl. Ah, you know, Pauline, I-- they gave us sometimes about two potatoes or three potatoes a day, sometimes instead of soup with a piece of bread. And when I heard my father was there, I said to the-- a friend of mine, you still got the two, three potatoes with you? Can you please give it to me? When I'll get tomorrow my share, I'll give back to you. Because I wanted to give it to my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I took the buckets with another girl friend, and we went. And I saw him. He saw me. And there were around a lot of Germans with the dogs. And I was so scared. And I took the-- the three potatoes and I threw it, just like that. And there were a few men there. Everybody took one. I don't know if my father took one. They were, everybody, so hungry. They will die for-- for anything. I was start to cry. And my father start to cry. And then I couldn't speak to him. My heart was beating. My heart was hurting. I want to go and kiss him. I want to talk to him, and I couldn't do that. I couldn't do it. I knew it, because he will be punished, and I will be punished. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I heard that he die for diarrhea. He was lying on the floor for many days. Nobody came to him. Nobody did anything to him. And he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was that he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the one-- one-- it was one day when I was in concentration camp. I don't remember exactly. Because sometimes they used to ask men to come and fix something, because something was broken. They used to bring men, because we were only women there. There were no men at all. And then one day, again, I saw my brother, my brother. And he was standing on the queue with other men, just passing through. And they stopped where my-- my barrack was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was telling to the other girls, can you please call Lola? Call Lola, you know? And I came running. And I saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, eat what they're giving to you. Don't look if it's good, no good. Please eat it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is the last time I saw my brother too. And he died too. I didn't see him anymore. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be honest with you, not so good. First of all, they gave us a little bit of soup with a little piece of bread all day. We didn't have much. That's all what you get. Eat it or not eat it, that's all what you get, a bit of soup and a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes I saw-- because in concentration camp they had women dressed in German uniforms. They were very bad, very bad. They used to smack women, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw one day one German woman was hitting a woman. She-- she died. She was-- she had a stick, you know, those-- and she-- that poor woman, she got so much smack, she died. She killed her with a stick. And every day, Pauline, I saw so many dead people on the floor. In the corner they used to throw people one on top of the other, like animals. Look, to see this every day, every day, every day people would die, every day. Stacks, stacks, stacks of-- of-- of dead bodies, I saw every day. In the nighttime there used to come a truck, pick this-- the-- the bodies up, and took them to the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about God at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this time, I said, who is God? Where is he? And I was thinking, does the world know what we're going through here? Does America know what they're doing to us? Why they let this happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What we-- what did we done to this world to deserve that? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But nobody help us. Nobody. Were any of the Jewish high holidays acknowledged at all in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, Pauline?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know when it Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. People were suffering so much that they didn't have the-- the heads for anything. They only wanted to survive. The only-- the only thing I want them to do is just to survive. Maybe some people thought about it. Maybe, yes. I didn't, because my suffering was too much. I couldn't-- I couldn't remember anything anymore. My-- my-- my mind went that I-- I couldn't remember nothing. But maybe other people that were a bit older than me, maybe they thought about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Birkenau for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took us-- not all of us, some of us. They took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this selection made?

speaker: interviewee
text: By pointing out. By pointing out. The-- the officers, the general officers they are there, they pointing, you know? They-- you and you that you're going there and there and there, you know? And we went to Auschwitz. And they called White Canada. I didn't know what White Canada is, and I didn't know where I'm going. White Canada was where the crematoriums was-- was. The crematoriums was there. The barracks where we supposed to be was here, exactly opposite.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we have to do? Every day came from all around the world people, transport people. A lot of people, many people.

speaker: interviewee
text: German officers, they always used to stay on the corner, about maybe 6 7, 8, 10 German soldiers-- not soldiers, officers, standing on the corner and watching the people coming in from many countries, many countries. Children, mothers, fathers, grandfathers, grandmothers, together. We were not supposed to go out from the-- from the barracks, because they were afraid that we will tell them where they're going. We used to watch through the window. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day I looked. I saw an old man with a beard. He was waiting ahead. And he passed through the German officers. And the poor man took his hat off to greet them. My heart was bleeding. My heart was hurting, because I knew it, where this old man will go, to the crematorium. And he gives respect to the German officer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was very hard to take, very hard to take. And that was coming day after day, people coming nonstop. Nonstop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then when they went in, they-- they took them to a room. They undressed themself. And they send them to the-- to the-- where the gas rooms were. They gas the people first, and then they took them to the oven. How they took to the oven, because we could hear the screams. That was unbearable. Pauline, that was day, night, day, and night, hearing those screaming. Sometimes I can hear it here today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Women, children, gas-- because we were very close to the crematorium. Then they took some young boys, young men, Jewish people, to take the bodies [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the-- from the-- from the-- the gas rooms and take the bodies and put them inside the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every week [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they used to take those boys, put it on the wall, and shoot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why they did that? Because they didn't know the world to know what's happening there. After another week, brought other few boys, new ones. Every week they had to change those boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were from all the country in the world. I mean-- and every time before they went to be shot, they used to-- used to sing the anthem from their own country, because they knew they was going to be shot and they want to-- to sing it. And they sing the anthem from their own country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- then we knew it, what's going to happen. Soon we heard the anthem, we knew there was going to be shots. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every-- and every time we-- we knew it, that-- that there is singing the anthem, here we knew it was going to be those shots again. And that was going on-- every week they used to change those boys, because they were afraid in case some of them will be alive one day, and they will tell the story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the screams, that was unbearable. I couldn't take it anymore. It was much too much. And every day coming, people and people. I don't know, so many people. There were six million Jews, they say, coming all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what else we did? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After they cremated the-- the people, they brought the clothing to us. That's what we were there for. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They told us to take the-- the clothing, tear them apart, because they knew Jewish people, they got jewelry there. Lola, this is the end of Tape 2. Thank you.
